retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The upper and lower lips are known as, respectively, labium superius oris and labium inferius oris. Both the upper and lower lip contain mucosal membrane, vermilion, and cutaneous surfaces. While considerations of the lips are often centered on the vermilion zone, the upper lip extends from the nasolabial folds to the inferior margin of the nose, and the lower lip encompasses the region between the lateral commissures and the labiomental crease of the chin. The upper and lower lips intersect at the mouth angle, referred to as the commissure. This is the point at which several muscles involved in lip movement attach.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lips are an essential aspect of the human face and play a critical role in facial expression, phonation, sensation, mastication, physical attraction, and intimacy. The upper and lower lips are known as, respectively, labium superius oris and labium inferius oris. Both the upper and lower lip contains mucosal membrane, vermilion, and cutaneous surfaces. This article briefly reviews the anatomy, neurovasculature, and musculature of the lips, as well as important surgical and clinical considerations regarding lip pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Assessment -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Upper Lip Bite Test
retrieved_docs.content: Patients can be asked to protrude the lower jaw or bite their upper lip. The upper lip bite test assesses the ability to place the lower incisors over the upper lip. This acts as a predictor of the ability to subluxate the mandible during laryngoscopy. The grading system is as follows: Grade 1: the patient can fully cover the upper lip with the lower incisors Grade 2: the patient can partially cover the upper lip with the lower incisors Grade 3: the patient cannot reach the upper lip with the lower teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Structure and Function -- Lower Face
retrieved_docs.content: The lower face starts superiorly at the upper lip and ends inferiorly at the lower border of the chin. The lateral border of the lower face is made up of the angle of the mandible. The lower face region contains the lips, chin, and jaws. Lips

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The upper lip is characterized by a symmetrical pair of paramedian vertical philtral ridges bordering the central depression known as the philtrum, directly below the nasal septum. The philtral ridges and the philtrum are formed by a unique collection of dermal collagen and dense elastic tissue. As elasticity is diminished with age, the philtrum takes on a less prominent appearance. The philtrum is believed to serve as a supply of additional skin to be recruited for oral movements requiring stretching of the upper lip. The inferior margin of the philtrum forms the downward arch of the cupid’s bow, while the underlying fleshy fullness is known as the tubercle or procheilon. Outlining the vermilion borders of the upper and lower lips is a 2 to 3 mm pale convexity known as the white roll, formed by the bulging of the orbicularis oris muscle lying beneath. The upper and lower lips connect to the gums by the frenulum labii superioris and frenulum labii inferioris, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Structure and Function -- Lower Face
retrieved_docs.content: In the lower face region, the lips are the most noticeable structures in the area. The lips divide into the upper and lower lips. The function of the lips is for the articulation of speech, eating, kissing, and sensory structures. The orbicularis oris muscle surrounds the lips. The orbicularis oris muscle is a sphincter muscle that originates from the mandible and maxilla then inserts onto the skin of the lips. The action of the orbicularis oris muscle is to alter the shapes of the lips for eating, speaking, kissing, and more. In the lower face region, the risorius muscle inserts into modiolus at the angle of the mouth but originates from the parotid fascia. The action of the risorius muscle is to pull the angles of the mouth back. Most of the muscles in the lower face region will act predominantly on the lower lip. The medial region of the lower lip has two sets of paired muscles. These muscles are the mentalis and the depressor labii inferioris muscle. The mentalis muscle originates from the anterior mandible and inserts onto the chin. The action of the mentalis muscle is to protrude the lower lip, elevate, and wrinkle the skin of the jaw, while the depressor labii inferioris muscle originates from the mandible and inserts onto the lower lip. The action of the depressor labii inferioris muscle is to depress the lips. At the angles of the mouth, the depressor anguli oris muscle lies. The depressor anguli oris muscle originates from the tubercle of the mouth and inserts into the modiolus of the mouth. The action of the depressor anguli oris muscle is to depress the angle of the mouth.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: An important goal of lip reconstruction is to maintain the competence of the oral seal to allow for mastication and phonation. Surgical repair of lesions of the lips poses a specific challenge as significant aesthetic defects are possible with even minor disruptions of the natural vermilion border, cupid’s bow, and white roll. All incisions should be limited to within a single cosmetic unit to minimize the appearance of scars. The cosmetic units of the lips include a single unit comprising the lower lip and 1 medial and 2 lateral units of the upper lip. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Facial Expression Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The risorius is located around the oral commissure from lateral to medial along the lower lip, pulling the mouth's corner directly outward. The deeper buccinator tightens the cheek to prevent food pocketing while chewing. The depressor anguli oris (also known as the triangularis) is a continuation of the platysma muscle, pulling down on the corner of the mouth. Just medial to the triangularis is the depressor labii inferioris, which helps dilate the mouth and everts the lower lip when pouting.

retrieved_docs.title: Lip Implants -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The lips divide into “red” and “white” components, largely as a historical artifact of Euro-centric publications in the aesthetic surgery literature. As these terms are well-entrenched in the medical literature we will use them here for the sake of brevity and comparability to existing publications, to facilitate education and understanding. The red lip is a mucous membrane (representing the wet and dry vermilion; the actual coloration varies according to the genetic background of each patient), while the white lip (cutaneous lip, also variable in accordance with the background of each patient) is a cutaneous structure. The vermilion border represents the mucocutaneous junction (i.e., the demarcation between the red and white lip). The red lip further subdivides into a dry and wet portion, with the former located along the anterosuperior portion of the red lip. This line of demarcation represents the plane of apposition of the lips: the dry portion is external and visible when the mouth is closed and at rest, while the wet is internalized. The upper lip is composed of the philtrum and tubercle centrally, the paired philtral columns laterally, and the white roll of the vermilion border. The orbicularis oris muscle, which is innervated by the motor branches of the facial nerve, maintains oral competence by acting as a circumoral sphincter. The horizontal fibers of this muscle attach to the modiolus and philtral columns, with contraction producing a tightening of the upper lip. [1] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Lip -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft lip usually develops at the junction between the lateral and central segments of the upper lip. The cleft usually affects the upper lip and may extend into the maxilla and palate.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Structure and Function -- Middle Face
retrieved_docs.content: The muscular layer of the cheek contains many muscles. The cheek contains a muscle called the levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle. The levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle demarcates the medial part of the cheek and separates it from the nose. The levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle starts from the medial orbital wall, runs along the nostrils, and attaches to the upper lip. The action of the levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle is to elevate the upper lip and flares the nostrils. Just lateral to the levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle is the levator labii superioris muscle. The levator labii superioris labii muscle originates from the infraorbital margin and insets at the upper lip. The levator labii superioris muscle's action is to elevate the upper lip. Slightly lateral to the levator labii superioris muscle is the zygomaticus minor and major muscles. The zygomaticus minor and major muscles originate from the zygomatic angle. The zygomaticus minor muscle inserts into the skin of the upper lip. The action of the zygomaticus minor muscle is to elevate the upper lip. As for the zygomaticus major muscle, it inserts into the modiolus of the mouth. The action of the zygomaticus major muscle is to draw the angle of the mouth superiorly and laterally. There is a small muscle lateral to the zygomaticus major muscle called the levator anguli oris muscle. The levator anguli oris muscle originates from the maxilla and inserts into the modiolus of the mouth. The action of the levator anguli oris muscle is to assist in smiling by elevation of the angle of the mouth. Underneath, the zygomaticus muscles lie the buccinator muscle. The buccinator muscle originates from the alveolar process of the maxilla and mandible, buccinator crest, and temporomandibular joint. The action of the buccinator muscle is to compress the food against the buccal mucosa during mastication. Laterally, to the buccinator and zygomaticus muscle is the masseter muscle. The masseter muscle originates from the zygomatic arch and the maxillary process on the zygomatic bone, and then it inserts at the ramus of the mandible. The action of the masseter muscle is to elevate and protrude the mandible during mastication. The masseter muscle and the region anterior the ear contains the parotid gland superficially. The parotid gland produces digestive enzymes and is the structure the facial nerve penetrates before it divides into five nerve branches. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Monitoring -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Upper Lip Bite Test: The upper lip bite test involves asking the patient to bite their upper lip with their lower incisors. The test has a sensitivity of 68% to 93% and a specificity of 82% to 97%.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Oral Cavity (Mouth)  -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: The upper lip form during early embryogenesis from the maxillary, lateral nasal, and medial nasal facial prominences. These facial prominences develop from anteriorly migrating neural crest cells in combination with mesoderm and the head ectoderm. They then undergo growth and expansion, eventually leading to their fusion and the formation of facial structures. [4] The mandibular processes fuse to form the lower lip.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During the development of the fetus, the external human face starts to develop during the fourth through sixth weeks. The face derives from the first two pharyngeal arches, neural crest cells, frontonasal prominence, medial nasal prominence, oropharyngeal membrane, and lateral nasal prominence. During week four of development, the oropharyngeal membrane breaks down to create the oral cavity. The frontonasal prominences develop into the forehead, bridge of the nose, medial nasal prominences, and lateral nasal prominences. The medial nasal prominences will further develop into the primary palate, philtrum, upper four incisors, and parts of the jaw. The lateral nasal prominences develop into the sides of the nose. The first pharyngeal arch will form the cheeks, lateral upper lip, lateral upper jaw, and secondary palate. The second pharyngeal arch will form the lower lip and jaw. All these structures form bilaterally and migrate toward the midline before fusing. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Structure and Function -- Lower Face
retrieved_docs.content: The bony structures of the lips are made from the maxilla superiorly and the mandible inferiorly. Chin and Jaw

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The muscles of the mouth constitute a complex group of muscles that work together to perform various functions such as speaking, suckling, and chewing [10] [11] : Zygomaticus major - involved in pulling the mouth corner superior and laterally Zygomaticus minor - involved in pulling the upper lip superiorly Depressor labii inferioris - involved in pulling the lower lip inferior and laterally Levator labii superioris - elevates the upper lip Levator anguli oris - raising the angle of the mouth Buccinator - involved in suckling and chewing Orbicularis oris - involved in protruding the lips, such as in the act of sucking Mentalis - involved in the elevation and protrusion of the lower lips, such as when a patient drinks Platysma - involved in the depression of the mandible The intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue The four primary muscles of mastication include the temporalis, medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid, and masseter muscles. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: The mentum presentation of the fetus, in which the fetal chin is the leading part during delivery, is classified as a مّجيء وَجهي (face presentation). Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2036
retrieved_docs.content: with mediastinal exploration by transthoracic approach.” Beyond these operative definitions, Excision is identified as a “method_of” the broader group “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” linking it to procedures performed in the upper aerodigestive tract. Additionally, Excision has a relational connection to “plastic procedures on nose,” indicating that it is relevant to, and possibly integrated with, aesthetic or reconstructive nasal surgeries. Overall, Excision serves as an umbrella term for numerous surgical interventions across multiple specialties—including hepatobiliary, neurosurgical, ophthalmic, spinal, endocrine, and breast surgery—while deliberately omitting certain extensive laminectomy techniques, and it is positioned within the context of therapeutic and plastic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Digestive System is organized under broader parent categories that include neoplasms of unspecified nature or uncertain behavior and malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث). Within this hierarchy, the entity Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is linked to the concept of a malignant secondary tumor in other specified sites (ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى) and is identified as a malignant tumor of the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان). The classification also aligns malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) with the same secondary‑tumor mapping, indicating that the Arabic description corresponds to malignant secondary tumors in other locations. Melanomas of the skin are recognized as a subtype of malignant skin cancer and, through an inverse hierarchical relationship, are associated with malignant lip cancer in certain taxonomies. This integrated structure demonstrates how specific tumor types such as metastatic sublingual gland neoplasms and cutaneous melanomas are nested under broader parent categories and how multilingual mappings connect equivalent diagnostic terms across English and Arabic nomenclatures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

Mapping from malignant skin cancer to secondary malignant tumors in specific locations further illustrates the connection between primary skin lesions and their metastatic counterparts, with documented finding sites such as “ret.” Together, these hierarchical, mapping and morphological relationships provide a comprehensive framework for precise coding, diagnosis, and patient‑centered management of primary and metastatic cancers across multiple organ systems.

The SMQ non‑hematologic malignant diseases classification also includes metastatic cancer (السرطان النقيلي), squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck (سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق), lung cancer arising in non‑smokers (سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة), metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية), metastatic glandular carcinoma (سرطانة غدية نقيلية), malignant tumor of the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), and melanomas of the skin. Melanomas are members of the SMQ and are identified as a subtype of malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث); they are mapped from ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى, illustrating overlap between primary skin malignancies and secondary malignant tumor classifications. Melanomas share a pathological relationship with malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث) and may present with ocular involvement (retina). Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical framework
